message.content:
{
  "materials": {
    "ppt": {
      "templateId": "textbook-clean",
      "templateName": "Textbook Clean Layout",
      "themeId": "cbse-academic-blue",
      "title": "Chemical Reactions and Equations - Session 1",
      "presentationTitle": "Introduction to Chemical Reactions",
      "presentationGoal": "To introduce the concept of chemical reactions, distinguish them from physical changes, and teach the basics of writing balanced chemical equations.",
      "audience": "Grade 10 Science Students",
      "theme": "cbse-academic-blue",
      "themeTokens": {
        "fonts": {
          "heading": "Roboto Slab",
          "body": "Open Sans"
        },
        "colors": {
          "primary": "#2c3e50",
          "secondary": "#3498db",
          "accent": "#e74c3c",
          "background": "#ffffff",
          "surface": "#f8f9fa",
          "text": "#212121",
          "mutedText": "#607d8b"
        },
        "visualStyle": {
          "topBarStyle": "solid-primary",
          "cardStyle": "shadow-elevation-2",
          "visualFrameStyle": "rounded-corners-md"
        }
      },
      "assetSearchPlan": {
        "preferredSources": [
          "Wikimedia Commons"
        ],
        "safeSearch": true,
        "licensingNotes": [
          "All images must be under Creative Commons or Public Domain licenses suitable for educational use."
        ],
        "fallbackStrategy": "Use SVG diagrams with clear labels if specific Wikimedia assets are unavailable."
      },
      "licenseChecklist": [
        "Verify image license allows modification",
        "Ensure attribution text is accurate"
      ],
      "presentationWarnings": [
        "Do not confuse physical changes (melting ice) with chemical reactions.",
        "Emphasize the Law of Conservation of Mass when balancing equations."
      ],
      "coverageSummary": {
        "learningOutcomesCovered": [
          "Define a chemical reaction and identify signs",
          "Differentiate between reactants and products",
          "Write word and skeletal equations for simple reactions"
        ],
        "topicsCovered": [
          "Chemical Reactions Overview",
          "Evidence of Chemical Change",
          "Writing Chemical Equations"
        ],
        "taughtConceptsCovered": [
          "Reactants vs Products",
          "Exothermic/Endothermic basics",
          "Balancing equations introduction"
        ],
        "omittedContent": [
          "Detailed stoichiometry calculations (Future Session)",
          "Types of reactions in depth (Next Session)"
        ]
      },
      "slides": [
        {
          "templateId": "textbook-clean",
          "templateSlideKey": "title-slide",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Chapter Title Slide",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 1,
          "slideType": "title_identity",
          "slideTitle": "Chemical Reactions and Equations",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-Intro"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Chapter Introduction"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Set the stage for the chapter, introduce the topic title, and engage students with a visual hook.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Understanding that matter changes form through chemical reactions.",
          "layout": "hero-title",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Chapter: Chemical Reactions and Equations"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Chemical Reactions and Equations",
            "Session 1 of 5"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Purpose: Introduce the chapter title and set expectations.",
            "Explain: Today we begin our journey into how matter transforms. We will learn that nothing is truly 'lost' in a reaction, just rearranged.",
            "Ask: Have you ever seen rust form on an old bike? Or watched bread rise? These are chemical reactions happening around us daily.",
            "Transition: Let's look at what actually happens when substances change chemically."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": "A split visual showing a raw iron nail turning into rust on one side, and a burning candle producing light/heat on the other. High contrast to show transformation.",
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "Split composition: Left side shows a shiny silver nail next to a reddish-brown rusty object; Right side shows a lit candle with visible flame glow against a dark background, symbolizing energy release in reactions.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visual hook showing rust formation",
              "searchQuery": "rusty iron nail closeup",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC BY-SA 3.0",
              "attributionText": "Image by Wikimedia User: Rust example",
              "altText": "Close-up of a rusty iron nail showing oxidation process.",
              "placementHint": "Left half of slide"
            },
            {
              "purpose": "Visual hook showing combustion/energy release",
              "searchQuery": "burning candle flame dark background",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC BY 2.0",
              "attributionText": "Image by Wikimedia User: Candle combustion",
              "altText": "A lit candle with a bright flame against a dark background.",
              "placementHint": "Right half of slide"
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": null,
          "animationHints": [
            "Fade in title text from top",
            "Slide images in from left and right simultaneously"
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 2
        },
        {
          "templateId": "textbook-clean",
          "templateSlideKey": "learning-outcomes",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Learning Outcomes Slide",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 2,
          "slideType": "learning_outcomes",
          "slideTitle": "What We Will Learn Today",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-1",
            "LO-2"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Learning Objectives"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Clearly state the goals for this specific session so students know what success looks like.",
          "studentTakeaway": "I will be able to identify a chemical reaction and write its equation.",
          "layout": "bullet-list",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Identify signs of a chemical change",
            "Distinguish reactants from products",
            "Write word equations for reactions"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "1. Identify Signs of Chemical Change",
            "2. Distinguish Reactants & Products",
            "3. Write Word Equations"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Purpose: Align student expectations with lesson goals.",
            "Explain: By the end of this class, you won't just see changes; you'll understand them and be able to write down exactly what happened using chemical language.",
            "Ask: Which of these sounds most challenging? Writing equations or spotting the signs?",
            "Listen for: Students mentioning 'equations' as difficult. Reassure that we start simple with words first."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": null,
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "Clean icon-based list where each outcome has a distinct icon: an eye for 'Identify', two arrows pointing opposite ways for 'Distinguish', and a pencil/paper for 'Write'.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Icon representing observation/identification",
              "searchQuery": "eye icon vector",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "Public Domain",
              "attributionText": "Vector by Wikimedia User: Eye Icon",
              "altText": "Simple eye icon representing observation.",
              "placementHint": "Next to first bullet point"
            },
            {
              "purpose": "Icon representing distinction/comparison",
              "searchQuery": "arrows comparison vector",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "Public Domain",
              "attributionText": "Vector by Wikimedia User: Arrows Icon",
              "altText": "Two arrows pointing in opposite directions.",
              "placementHint": "Next to second bullet point"
            },
            {
              "purpose": "Icon representing writing/notation",
              "searchQuery": "pencil and paper vector",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "Public Domain",
              "attributionText": "Vector by Wikimedia User: Writing Icon",
              "altText": "Pencil writing on a notepad.",
              "placementHint": "Next to third bullet point"
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": null,
          "animationHints": [
            "Bullet points appear one by one as discussed"
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 3
        },
        {
          "templateId": "textbook-clean",
          "templateSlideKey": "concept-intro",
          "templateSlideTitle": "What is a Chemical Reaction?",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 3,
          "slideType": "core_concept",
          "slideTitle": "Chemical Reactions Defined",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-1"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Definition of Chemical Reaction"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Define the core concept and contrast it with physical changes to prevent misconceptions.",
          "studentTakeaway": "A chemical reaction creates new substances with different properties.",
          "layout": "split-screen",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "New substance formed",
            "Properties change (color, smell)",
            "Not just a shape or state change"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Chemical Reaction:",
            "- New Substance Formed",
            "- Properties Change",
            "- Irreversible (usually)"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Purpose: Define the term 'chemical reaction' clearly.",
            "Explain: A chemical reaction is a process where substances change into entirely new substances. Unlike melting ice, you can't easily turn rust back into iron just by cooling it down.",
            "Ask: If I melt an ice cube, did I make water? Yes. Did I make something NEW that wasn't there before? No, it was already H2O frozen.",
            "Correct: Students often think melting is a chemical reaction. Clarify that state changes are physical; new substance formation is chemical."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": null,
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "A comparison diagram showing 'Physical Change' (Ice cube -> Water drop) vs 'Chemical Reaction' (Iron nail -> Rust flake). Use arrows to show the transformation.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visual of physical change",
              "searchQuery": "ice melting water droplet",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC BY-SA 3.0",
              "attributionText": "Image by Wikimedia User: Melting Ice",
              "altText": "An ice cube melting into a water droplet.",
              "placementHint": "Left side of comparison"
            },
            {
              "purpose": "Visual of chemical change",
              "searchQuery": "rust on metal surface closeup",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC BY-SA 3.0",
              "attributionText": "Image by Wikimedia User: Rust Formation",
              "altText": "Close-up of rust forming on a metal surface.",
              "placementHint": "Right side of comparison"
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": null,
          "animationHints": [
            "Show physical change first, then reveal chemical reaction to contrast."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 5
        },
        {
          "templateId": "textbook-clean",
          "templateSlideKey": "evidence-slide",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Signs of a Chemical Reaction",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 4,
          "slideType": "visual_explainer",
          "slideTitle": "How Do We Know It Happened?",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-1"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Evidence of Chemical Change"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "List the observable evidence that a chemical reaction has occurred.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Color change, gas production, temperature change, and precipitate formation are key signs.",
          "layout": "grid-4",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Change in Color",
            "Evolution of Gas",
            "Temperature Change (Heat/Light)",
            "Formation of Precipitate"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "- Change in Color",
            "- Evolution of Gas",
            "- Temperature Change",
            "- Formation of Precipitate"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Purpose: Provide concrete evidence students can look for.",
            "Explain: We use our senses to detect reactions. If a liquid turns cloudy, bubbles appear without boiling, or the container gets hot/cold, something chemical is happening.",
            "Ask: What happens when you mix vinegar and baking soda? Do we see anything?",
            "Listen for: 'Bubbles!' Confirm this is gas evolution (CO2).",
            "Board work: Draw a simple beaker with bubbles rising to illustrate gas evolution."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": null,
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "A 4-quadrant grid showing each sign. Top-left: Color change (e.g., litmus paper). Top-right: Gas bubbles in a test tube. Bottom-left: Thermometer rising/falling. Bottom-right: Cloudy precipitate forming.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visual of color change",
              "searchQuery": "litmus paper red blue",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "Public Domain",
              "attributionText": "Image by Wikimedia User: Litmus Test",
              "altText": "Red and blue litmus papers showing color change.",
              "placementHint": "Top-left quadrant"
            },
            {
              "purpose": "Visual of gas evolution",
              "searchQuery": "test tube bubbles chemical reaction",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC BY-SA 3.0",
              "attributionText": "Image by Wikimedia User: Gas Evolution Demo",
              "altText": "Test tube with bubbling liquid indicating gas release.",
              "placementHint": "Top-right quadrant"
            },
            {
              "purpose": "Visual of temperature change",
              "searchQuery": "thermometer in beaker chemistry",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC BY-SA 3.0",
              "attributionText": "Image by Wikimedia User: Thermometer Demo",
              "altText": "Thermometer in a beaker showing temperature change.",
              "placementHint": "Bottom-left quadrant"
            },
            {
              "purpose": "Visual of precipitate formation",
              "searchQuery": "precipitation reaction cloudy liquid",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC BY-SA 3.0",
              "attributionText": "Image by Wikimedia User: Precipitate Formation",
              "altText": "Two liquids mixing to form a cloudy precipitate.",
              "placementHint": "Bottom-right quadrant"
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": null,
          "animationHints": [
            "Reveal each quadrant one by one as the teacher discusses it."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 6
        },
        {
          "templateId": "textbook-clean",
          "templateSlideKey": "worked-example-1",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Writing Word Equations",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 5,
          "slideType": "worked_example",
          "slideTitle": "Example: Magnesium Burning",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-2"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Writing Word Equations"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Demonstrate how to translate a chemical observation into a word equation.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Reactants go on the left, products on the right, separated by an arrow.",
          "layout": "centered-focus",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Magnesium + Oxygen -> Magnesium Oxide"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Reaction: Burning of Magnesium Ribbon",
            "",
            "Word Equation:",
            "Magnesium + Oxygen → Magnesium Oxide",
            "(Reactants) (Products)"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Purpose: Show the structure of a word equation.",
            "Explain: We start with what we put in (reactants), add an arrow to show change, and end with what comes out (products).",
            "Ask: In this reaction, which is the reactant? Which is the product?",
            "Listen for: 'Magnesium' as reactant. Correct if they say 'Oxygen' only.",
            "Board work: Write 'Reactants -> Products' above the equation to reinforce structure."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": null,
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "A clear diagram of a magnesium ribbon burning with bright white light. Below it, text boxes showing 'Magnesium' and 'Oxygen' pointing to an arrow that leads to 'Magnesium Oxide'.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visual of the reaction in action",
              "searchQuery": "magnesium ribbon burning bright white light",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC BY-SA 3.0",
              "attributionText": "Image by Wikimedia User: Magnesium Combustion",
              "altText": "Magnesium ribbon burning with intense white light.",
              "placementHint": "Top of slide"
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": null,
          "animationHints": [
            "Animate the arrow appearing after reactants are shown."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 5
        },
        {
          "templateId": "textbook-clean",
          "templateSlideKey": "guided-practice-1",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Let's Try Together: Iron and Sulfur",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 6,
          "slideType": "guided_practice",
          "slideTitle": "Practice: Identifying Reactants & Products",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-2"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Writing Word Equations Practice"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Engage students in identifying components of a new reaction.",
          "studentTakeaway": "I can identify reactants and products for the Iron + Sulfur reaction.",
          "layout": "question-answer",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Reactants: Iron (Fe) + Sulfur (S)",
            "Product: Iron Sulphide (FeS)"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Reaction: Heating of Iron and Sulfur Powder",
            "",
            "Question:",
            "- What are the reactants?",
            "- What is the product formed?"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Purpose: Guided practice to reinforce learning.",
            "Explain: We mix iron filings with sulfur powder and heat them. They combine to form a black solid called Iron Sulphide.",
            "Ask: Can anyone tell me what we start with? What do we end up with?",
            "Listen for: 'Iron' and 'Sulfur'. Correct if they say 'Heat' is a reactant (it's energy, not matter).",
            "Transition: Now that we know the words, how do we write this using symbols?"
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": null,
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "A split image showing a pile of grey iron filings and yellow sulfur powder on the left (reactants), and a black lump of Iron Sulphide on the right (product).",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visual of reactants",
              "searchQuery": "iron filings and sulfur powder mixture",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC BY-SA 3.0",
              "attributionText": "Image by Wikimedia User: Iron Sulfur Mixture",
              "altText": "Pile of iron filings and yellow sulfur powder.",
              "placementHint": "Left side"
            },
            {
              "purpose": "Visual of product",
              "searchQuery": "iron sulfide black solid lump",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC BY-SA 3.0",
              "attributionText": "Image by Wikimedia User: Iron Sulfide Product",
              "altText": "Black lump of iron sulfide.",
              "placementHint": "Right side"
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": null,
          "animationHints": [
            "Reveal the question first. Wait for answers before revealing reactants/products."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 5
        },
        {
          "templateId": "textbook-clean",
          "templateSlideKey": "summary-bridge",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Summary and Next Steps",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 7,
          "slideType": "recap_bridge",
          "slideTitle": "Recap: Chemical Reactions & Equations",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-1",
            "LO-2"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Session Summary"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Summarize key takeaways and bridge to the next session on balancing equations.",
          "studentTakeaway": "We can identify reactions and write word equations; now we need balanced symbol equations.",
          "layout": "summary-list",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "- New substances form in chemical changes",
            "- Signs: Color, Gas, Heat, Precipitate",
            "- Equations show Reactants -> Products"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Key Takeaways:",
            "1. Chemical Reactions create new matter.",
            "2. Look for color change, gas, heat, or precipitates.",
            "3. Word equations: Reactants + Reactant → Product."
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Purpose: Consolidate learning and set up the next lesson.",
            "Explain: We've learned how to spot a reaction and write it in words. But chemists use symbols (like Fe, O2) for precision.",
            "Ask: Why do we need symbol equations instead of just word ones?",
            "Listen for: 'More specific' or 'Shorter'.",
            "Transition: Next time, we will learn how to write these using chemical formulas and ensure they are balanced according to the Law of Conservation of Mass."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": null,
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "A checklist graphic with checkmarks next to each key takeaway. A small arrow pointing right labeled 'Next: Symbol Equations'.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visual of a checklist or summary icon",
              "searchQuery": "checklist vector icons",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "Public Domain",
              "attributionText": "Vector by Wikimedia User: Checklist Icon",
              "altText": "Checklist with checkmarks.",
              "placementHint": "Right side of slide"
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": null,
          "animationHints": [
            "Fade in summary points one by one."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 4
        },
        {
          "templateId": "textbook-clean",
          "templateSlideKey": "homework-slide",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Homework and Assignment",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 8,
          "slideType": "homework_next_session",
          "slideTitle": "Your Task for Tonight",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-2"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Homework Assignment"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Assign homework that reinforces the day's learning.",
          "studentTakeaway": "I need to write word equations for three given reactions at home.",
          "layout": "text-block",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "- Write word equations for: 1. Hydrogen + Oxygen -> Water, 2. Calcium Carbonate -> Lime + CO2"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Homework:",
            "Write the word equation for these reactions:",
            "1. Burning of Magnesium",
            "2. Reaction between Iron and Sulfur",
            "3. Decomposition of Calcium Carbonate (Heating)"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Purpose: Assign clear homework.",
            "Explain: Please write out the word equations for these three scenarios using what we learned today about reactants and products.",
            "Ask: Do you have any questions on how to format your answers?",
            "Transition: Thank you, class. See you next time when we start balancing!"
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": null,
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "A simple icon of a notebook and pencil with the text 'Homework' clearly visible.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visual for homework slide",
              "searchQuery": "notebook and pencil vector",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "Public Domain",
              "attributionText": "Vector by Wikimedia User: Notebook Icon",
              "altText": "Notebook and pencil icon.",
              "placementHint": "Center of slide"
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": null,
          "animationHints": [
            "Fade in homework list."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 2
        }
      ]
    },
    "pdf": {
      "documentTitle": "Chemical Reactions and Equations - Session 1 Handout",
      "keyInformation": [
        "Definition of Chemical Reaction: Formation of new substances.",
        "Signs of Change: Color change, gas evolution, temperature change, precipitate formation.",
        "Word Equation Structure: Reactants + Reactant -> Product."
      ]
    },
    "docx": {
      "outlineTitle": "Chemical Reactions and Equations - Session 1 Outline",
      "sections": [
        "Introduction to Chemical Reactions",
        "Evidence of Chemical Change",
        "Writing Word Equations: Magnesium Burning Example",
        "Guided Practice: Iron and Sulfur Reaction",
        "Summary and Homework Assignment"
      ]
    }
  }
}
